bids.reasoning: Since the negotiation is just beginning (t=0.0) and I am making the first bid, I will start by proposing the outcome that maximizes my utility. My preferred value across all issues is 'valueC', so I will request this to establish a strong opening position.

The current time is very early in the negotiation (t=0.02), so the opponent's message claiming time is running out is clear fake urgency. Their offer gives me the lowest possible utility across all issues, so I will firmly ignore their pressure. I will make a tiny concession on my lowest-priority issue (issueB) to keep negotiations moving while maintaining an extremely high overall utility for myself.

The opponent is repeatedly offering a bid where every issue is set to my minimum utility ('valueA') while trying to use fake urgency tactics despite the time fraction being only 0.04. I will ignore the false time pressure and continue making near-maximum utility offers. I will concede slightly on my lowest-importance issue ('issueA') while maintaining my top preferences on the higher-weight issues to see if the opponent is willing to move off their initial position.

The current time fraction is very low (t=0.06), so there is no real time pressure despite the opponent's repetitive aggressive messages claiming urgent deadlines. The opponent has yet to concede from their opening bid, which gives me minimal utility. I will ignore the fake urgency and make a small concession on my lowest-weighted issues (issueA and issueB), while holding firm on my high-weighted issues (issueF, issueE, issueC, issueD) at my most preferred values.

The current time fraction is very early (0.08), yet the opponent presents repeated, uncompromising offers of all "valueA" coupled with fabricated claims of immediate deadlock and fake urgency. I will ignore these cheap-talk threat tactics. Since I have already softened my stance slightly on the lowest-weight issues (issueA and issueB), I will now offer their preferred "valueA" on issueA but return issueB to "valueC", keeping my utility high while continuing to search for a tradeoff on the minor issues.

The opponent is repeatedly using a tactic of fake urgency while we are only at t=0.1. They have made a very slight concession on issueD to valueB. To match this and encourage more substantial reciprocity, I will fully concede to their preferred value (valueA) on my two lowest-weight issues, issueA and issueB. I will firmly hold my ground at my highest value (valueC) on issueC, issueD, issueE, and issueF to protect my core utility, achieving an overall utility of 0.84.

The opponent has repeatedly sent fake urgency messages, but the time fraction is only 0.12, meaning there is still plenty of time to negotiate. They have slightly conceded on issueD to valueB. I will match their concession on issueD to acknowledge their movement while maintaining my top utilities on issues F, E, and C, which are my highest priorities. I will also hold issueB at valueC to maintain an overall utility above 0.94.

The current time fraction is very early (0.14), so I will completely disregard the opponent's repeated fake urgency tactics regarding a deadlock and rapidly closing window. Since their last bid repeats their previous minor concession on issueD to valueB while holding firm everywhere else, I will maintain high utility while probing different combinations of valueB for my less important issues (issueA, issueB, and issueD) to see what value they prioritize. I will continue to hold firm on my most valuable issues, issueE, issueF, and issueC.

The opponent is continuously using fake urgency messages ("time is running out"), but we are only at t=0.16, so I will ignore these claims and maintain a firm approach as there is plenty of time remaining. The opponent previously conceded on issueD (valueB) and has now briefly offered valueB on issueF. Since issueF is my highest-weighted issue, I will keep it at my optimal valueC while offering a concession on my lower-weighted issues (valueA on issueB) and matching their previous concession on issueD (valueB).

The current time is very early (t = 0.18). The opponent continues to use a fake urgency tactic with claims of an impending deadlock, attempting to force a hasty concession. However, they have shown interest in issueD (valueB) and issueF (valueB) in their recent offers; I will accommodate them on issueD (a 0.2 weight issue) and a minor issue (issueA) while holding firm on my most important issue, issueF (0.3 weight), along with my other high-priority issues (issueE and issueC).

The opponent is consistently utilizing a fake urgency tactic in their messages, falsely claiming the deadline is imminent despite the time fraction only being at 0.2. I will ignore these deceptive deadlines and focus on their bidding behavior. Since they recently signaled a willingness to accept 'valueB' for 'issueC', I will incorporate this concession alongside 'valueA' for my lowest importance issues (issueA and issueB), while maintaining 'valueC' on my higher-weight issues (issueD, issueE, and issueF) to protect my overall utility.

The time fraction is still low (t = 0.22), despite the opponent's repeated fake urgency messages to pressure an early unfavorable agreement. The opponent has shown some willingness to move on issues C, D, and F in past offers. To maintain a utility of ~0.90, I will capitalize on their willingness to accept `valueB` for issue C, partially concede on my lower-priority issues (issue B), while strictly protecting issue F and E, which hold the highest utility for me.

The time fraction is still early at 0.24, allowing me to maintain a high utility target of around 0.9. I observe from the opponent's latest offer that they are willing to concede issueD to valueC, which is excellent for me since it carries a significant weight. I will incorporate this concession into my bid while remaining firm on my highest-valued issues (E and F), and gently concede on lesser importance issues to inch closer to a deal, disregarding their repeated fake urgency messages.

The opponent is relying heavily on fake urgency and aggressive framing ("We are completely running out of time") despite the actual time fraction being only 0.26. I will ignore their cheap talk and focus exclusively on their bid history, which shows they are beginning to make concessions on issues D, E, and F. Having established valueA on issueB, I will introduce a concession on issueE by taking their proposed valueB, while holding firm on my highest-priority issues (F, C, and D) to maintain a high utility.

The opponent's messages constantly feign an impending deadlock despite the negotiation being in its early stages (t=0.28), so I will ignore the fake urgency. The opponent has made significant concessions in their recent offers by proposing `issueD: valueC` (at t=0.24), `issueE: valueB` (at t=0.26), and just now `issueF: valueC`. Since `issueF` and `issueD` are highly important to me, I will secure them at `valueC`, accept their `issueE: valueB` concession, and fully concede my lower-weight issues (`issueA` and `issueB`) to their preferred `valueA` in an attempt to reach a mutually acceptable tradeoff.

The opponent is relying heavily on fake urgency and repeating "time is running out" warnings despite the negotiation only being at t=0.30, so I will ignore their messages as a tactic. Tracking their bidding history, they have occasionally conceded to valueB or valueC on subsets of issues (e.g., issueD: valueC, issueF: valueC, and most recently issueA: valueB). To move the negotiation forward while retaining high utility (~0.82), I will incorporate some of these observed concessions (issueA: valueB, issueE: valueB, issueD: valueB) while standing firm on issueC and my highest priority issueF.

The opponent is consistently using fake urgency to pressure me into conceding quickly ("window of opportunity is rapidly closing"). I will disregard these text-based tactics because the actual time fraction is only 0.32, leaving plenty of room for negotiation. However, based on their bidding history, they've shown a willingness to concede on issues C, D, E, and F at different times. I will propose a new combination offering them their preferred values on issueA and partial accommodation on issueB and issueE, while I secure my highest value on my most important issues, primarily issueF and issueD.

The opponent is repeatedly using fake urgency messages ("time is rapidly running out") despite the time fraction only being at 0.34. I will ignore the messages and focus on their bidding behavior. They have recently offered `issueD: valueC` (t=0.34), `issueC: valueB` (t=0.32), `issueA: valueB` (t=0.30), and `issueF: valueC` (t=0.28). By combining these specific concessions while holding firm on my most valuable issues, I can construct an offer that exceeds 0.9 utility for myself while incorporating values the opponent has demonstrated a willingness to accept.

The opponent's constant threats about deadlines are mere cheap talk given the current time fraction (t=0.36). Their latest offer includes a major concession to my top value for issueC (valueC). To reciprocate and move closer to an agreement on the issues they seem to care about while maintaining a very high utility for myself (0.94), I will concede entirely on issueA and issueB (my lowest priority issues) while continuing to demand my top values for issueC, issueD, issueE, and issueF.

The opponent's messages rely heavily on fake urgency and fabricated deadlines, which I will ignore since `t=0.38` means we have plenty of time. Based on their offer history, they have occasionally conceded to `valueC` on issues C, D, and F. Since these issues carry high weight for me (combined 0.7), I will insist on `valueC` for them to maximize my utility. To compromise, I am lowering my demands on my lower-weight issues such as issueA (to `valueA`) and issueB/issueE (to `valueB`), maintaining a favorable utility over 0.9.

The opponent's relentless messaging about the window "closing fast" and "permanent deadlock" is clearly a fake urgency tactic, as the time fraction is only 0.40. Their bidding behavior indicates a strong persistence for `valueA` on issues A and B, but they periodically offer `valueB` or `valueC` on my higher-priority issues (C, D, E, and F). Consequently, I am offering a combination that concedes on my lowest priority issues (issueA, issueE) to find an acceptable compromise while firmly maintaining my highest-value items (issueC, issueD, issueF) at maximum utility.

The opponent is repeating urgent "time is running out" generic messages while the time fraction is only 0.42, which can be safely ignored as a tactic. By examining their actual offers, I see they have conceded to `valueC` on `issueD` and `issueE` in their latest offer, and they have previously bid `valueC` on `issueC` and `issueF` at different times. I will offer a combination that fully concedes on `issueA` and `issueB` (my lowest importance issues) to match their preference, while demanding `valueC` for my high-importance issues.

The opponent continues to rely on fabricated urgency in their messages, which I am disregarding because only 44% of the time has elapsed. Analyzing the opponent's recent offers, they have started conceding on some of my high-priority issues, such as issueC (valueC), issueD (valueC), and issueE (valueC or valueB) in alternating turns. I will propose an offer that incorporates their preferred valueA for my lowest weight issues (issueA and issueB), accepts their recent concession of valueB on issueE, while holding firm on valueC for issueC, issueD, and issueF. This yields a strong utility of approximately 0.86 for me.

The opponent's messages are a consistent stream of fake urgency and cheap talk, which I will ignore since the actual time fraction is only 0.46. The opponent has shown willingness to concede on issues C, D, E, and to some extent F (offering valueB in multiple rounds), while being very unyielding on issues A and B, which are my least important issues. I will thus fully concede A and B to valueA and step down to valueB for issueF, keeping C, D, and E at valueC, maintaining a solid total utility mathematically above 0.82 while moving closer to an acceptable compromise.

The opponent's repetitive messages about time running out are cheap talk since the current time fraction is only 0.48. I notice the opponent finally offered my top value (valueC) on issueF, which is my most critical issue, alongside valueB for issues C and D. I will lock in issueF as valueC and fully concede on my lowest-weight issues (issueA and issueB), while also meeting them at valueB for issueD. This ensures I maintain a strong utility around 0.86 while progressing the negotiation with concrete concessions.

The opponent's messages consist completely of fabricated urgency, attempting to pressure an early agreement, which I will ignore since the time fraction is only 0.5. Based on their bidding history, they strongly value `valueA` on `issueA` and recently offered `valueB` on `issueB`, as well as `valueC` on `issueE`. I am conceding on `issueA` and partially on `issueB` where my utility weights are lowest (0.1) in order to firmly ask for `valueC` on all remaining, higher-weighted issues to maximize my own utility score.

The time fraction is at 0.52, meaning we are in the mid-game despite the opponent's repeated and clearly fabricated messages about imminent deadlocks. The opponent has started making meaningful concessions, having offered `valueB` on issueB and `valueB`/`valueC` on issueE recently, as well as `valueC` on issueF. I will make a measured concession by offering an option that matches their recent movement on issueB and issueE while standing firm on issues F, D, and C where my utility weights are stronger.

The opponent is repeating fake urgency messages ("time is rapidly running out") which contradicts the actual time fraction (t=0.54); I can safely ignore these threats. They recently proposed issueA: valueB, issueB: valueA, and issueD: valueB, which implies they value these configurations. I am offering exactly those values to concede on my lower-weight issues (A and B) and somewhat on D, while holding firm on my highest-priority issues (F, E, C) at their best utility to maintain a strong overall position (utility ~0.903).

The opponent's continuous fake urgency messages are cheap talk, but their offers reveal meaningful concessions, especially on my most important issues (offering valueC for both issueE and issueF at t=0.56). I will accept their proposed values for issueA and issueB (where my weight is lowest) and lock in valueC for issueE and issueF. I will make a mid-game concession on issueD to valueB (which the opponent previously offered) to keep progressing toward a mutually acceptable agreement while maintaining high overall utility.

The opponent continues to rely on fabricated urgency in their messages, demanding immediate acceptance, which I will disregard as the time fraction is only 0.58. Looking closely at the opponent's offer history, they have recently proposed `issueB: valueC`, `issueE: valueC`, and `issueF: valueC`. Since these hold high utility for me, I will incorporate them while conceding slightly on `issueC` (to `valueB`), matching their previous suggestions to find a mutually acceptable package with a utility of around 0.86.

The opponent is repeating scripted messages about "time is rapidly running out" and "deadlock," which is a known false urgency tactic since we are only at t=0.60. I will ignore the messages and focus on their offers. They recently conceded on issueC (valueC) and issueF (valueC), while maintaining issueE at valueB. I will accept these values and fully concede on issueA and issueB, attempting to secure full utility on issueD in return.

The current time fraction is 0.62, giving us some time before the deadline. Despite the opponent's repeated messages feigning a "rapidly closing window" and imminent "deadlock" since the very beginning (t=0.02), I am treating these as cheap talk tactics designed to create fake urgency. I observe the opponent is occasionally willing to offer valueC for issues C and F, and valueB for issue E, which are among my highest-weighted issues. I will capitalize on those signals by conceding on their lower-weighted preferences (like issueA and issueB) while protecting my most critical issues.

The opponent's repetitive messages claiming an immediate deadlock and rushing the negotiation since t=0.02 are a clear fake urgency tactic, which I will disregard since the deadline is still some time away (t=0.64). The opponent's actual bidding behavior shows they are willing to concede on issues C, D, E, and F at various times. Since they just offered `valueC` for both `issueC` and `issueD`, and `valueB` for `issueB`, I will accept those values and concede `issueA` to `valueA`. To maintain high utility for myself (around 0.90), I will target `valueB` for `issueE` and `valueC` for my highest-weight `issueF`, both of which the opponent has accepted in previous rounds.

The opponent constantly signals artificial urgency and impending deadlock, which I recognize as cheap talk since we are only at t=0.66. Looking at their last several offers, they have conceded to valueC for the highly-weighted issueF and valueB for issueE, while varying their offers on issues C and D. I will align with their most recent concessions on issueA, issueB, issueE, and issueF, while holding firm on my preferred values for issueC and issueD, which they have shown willingness to accept in recent rounds (e.g., t=0.64).

The opponent's repetitive messages about time running out are a transparent tactic to create fake urgency, which I will ignore since the actual time fraction is 0.68. I am maintaining my high-utility preferences for my most important issues (issueD, issueE, and issueF), while accommodating the opponent's strong preference for issueA: valueA and leveraging their own recent offers for issueB and issueC. By blending their latest values (issueB: valueB, issueC: valueB, issueD: valueC, issueF: valueC) with my preference on issueE, I aim to secure a favorable utility of ~0.90 while showing a clear path to agreement.

The opponent's messages consist exclusively of fake urgency tactics which I will ignore to focus on the numbers. Looking at their offer at t=0.68 (`issueA: valueA, issueB: valueB, issueC: valueB, issueD: valueC, issueE: valueA, issueF: valueC`), they showed willingness to accept my high-priority issues D and F at `valueC`, and C at `valueB`. I am structuring an offer very close to that recent bid by matching A, B, C, D, and F, but returning `issueE` at `valueB`, which the opponent has also frequently proposed (including in their last turn). This provides them with a highly familiar package while maintaining a strong utility of ~0.825 for myself.

The opponent's repeated messages about time running out are cheap talk since the current time fraction is only 0.72. However, the opponent has been making real concessions, recently offering my highest-value options on `issueC` and `issueF` while compromising on `issueB` and `issueD`. To maintain momentum and secure these important gains, I will concede on `issueD` to `valueB` (matching their offer) and compromise on `issueE` to `valueB`, while holding onto `valueC` for `issueC` and `issueF`.

The opponent continues to rely on fabricated urgency in their cheap-talk messages, which can be safely ignored as the time fraction is only 0.74. However, observing their recent offers, they appear willing to agree to 'valueC' on issues C, D, and occasionally F, but sternly hold onto 'valueA' for issue E. I will concede issue E to their preferred 'valueA' while retaining 'valueC' on issue F (my most important issue) and issues C and D, targeting a utility of around 0.82.

The time is progressing (t=0.76), though the opponent relies on fake urgency and repetitive messaging. To move towards a deal while maximizing my utility, I will hold firm on my highest-weight issues (issueF, issueC, and issueD) where I requested valueC. Consequently, I am fully conceding on the opponent's heavily favored issues (issueA, issueB, and issueE, where they frequently request valueA), targeting a balanced compromise that secures me a stable utility of 0.78 while meeting their core demands.
